nativeRange.habitat: Subtropical demersal species inhabiting sandy and seagrass bottoms, often adjacent to rocky reefs, from the intertidal zone to about 100 m depth. Endemic to eastern Australia (approximately 28°S to 38°S).

decorPreferences: Deep, soft sand substrate (4+ inches) to allow burying
Large open sandy footprint (front-to-back depth of at least 90 cm advised)
Smooth rockwork along tank perimeter only — avoid sharp or abrasive decor
Rounded tank corners preferred to reduce collision injuries
Minimal vertical structure; species occupies the bottom almost exclusively

diet.notes: In the wild, feeds mainly on crabs and shrimps and acts as an active benthic scavenger. In captivity, has been reported to accept cleaned squid, live or frozen ghost shrimp, fresh marine fish pieces, and scallops. New arrivals frequently refuse food initially; live prey is commonly used to stimulate the first feeding response.

goodWith: Large peaceful fish that occupy the upper water column
Tankmates too large to be consumed and not prone to fin-nipping
avoidWith: Crustaceans and small molluscs (will be hunted as prey)
Small fish that may fit in the ray's mouth
Other rays (territorial stress and injury risk in shared substrate)

summary: The Fiddler Stingray (Trygonorrhina fasciata), also known as the Eastern Fiddler Ray or Banjo Ray, is a subtropical Australian elasmobranch with bold dark-edged banding on a sandy-brown disc. It is generally regarded as an expert-only species due to its large adult size and demanding husbandry.
careNotes:
The Fiddler Stingray is a subtropical, demersal ray endemic to eastern Australian coastal waters and is generally considered suitable only for very large public-aquarium-style displays. Adults are reported to reach roughly 90–120 cm total length, which translates to a disc width on the order of 45–60 cm; a tank footprint with at least 90 cm of front-to-back depth and a long open sandy area is widely advised.

A deep, soft, fine-sand substrate is important to allow the species to bury itself for camouflage and to prevent abrasion injuries to the ventral surface; coarser or sharp substrates have been associated with abdominal scratching and subsequent infection. The tail bears a venomous defensive spine — handling and netting should be undertaken with care.

Like other elasmobranchs, the species is highly sensitive to copper-based medications and should never be exposed to them. Maintaining stable water chemistry and avoiding crowding with faster, more aggressive feeders is generally advised.

Cross-source disagreement was noted on temperature (LiveAquaria and AquariumDomain quote 22–26°C aquarium ranges, while FishBase reports a wild temperature range of about 17–22°C for the species' subtropical Australian distribution — averaged here to roughly 20–25°C; flagged for review). Sources also disagreed on maximum size (LiveAquaria/AquariumDomain ~36 in / 91 cm total length, FishBase 120 cm total length); a disc-width range of 45–60 cm was used to reflect husbandry-relevant body size. Minimum tank volume was set at 2000 L on the cautious side, given LiveAquaria's 500+ US gal (~1893 L) recommendation, AquariumDomain's 400 US gal (~1514 L) figure, and external references suggesting 3000 L for adults. Scientific name spelling follows FishBase (Trygonorrhina fasciata, with two r's) over LiveAquaria's older 'Trygonorhina' rendering; family is given as Trygonorrhinidae per current taxonomy rather than the older Rhinobatidae placement still cited by some retailers.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: The apparent temperature conflict between FishBase (wild range 17.3–22.5°C) and aquarium trade sources (LiveAquaria 72–78°F / 22.2–25.6°C; AquariumDomain 72–78°F / 22–26°C) is not a true contradiction. FishBase documents the full seasonal environmental range experienced across the species' distribution (28°S–38°S eastern Australia), which includes winter water temperatures well below what aquarists target. The captive husbandry consensus of 22–26°C correctly reflects the warmer portion of the wild range — the temperatures at which the species is active and feeding, not the cold seasonal extremes it merely tolerates in the wild. temperatureC has been updated from 20–25°C to 22–26°C to align with both retailer sources and the upper (active-season) portion of the FishBase wild range.
breedingNotes: Ovoviviparous; females produce golden egg capsules containing up to three embryos, giving birth to live young (typically 2–3 pups per cycle) at approximately 25 cm total length. Successful captive breeding has not been widely reported and is not considered practical for private aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary aquarium-trade care sheet. Provided tank size, water parameters, salinity, temperament, diet, and care level.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Trygonorrhinidae / Rhinopristiformes), maximum size (120 cm TL), wild temperature range (17.3–22.5°C), distribution (endemic to eastern Australia), depth range (0–100 m), and diet (crabs and shrimps). Discrepancy with retailer temperatures noted in careNotes.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Third source for tank size (400 US gal / ~1514 L), temperature (72–78°F / ~22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), specific gravity (1.020–1.025), care level (Expert), and behavior (uses sandy substrate for camouflage, can jump from open-top tanks).

fish.breedingNotes: Ovoviviparous; not reliably bred in private aquariums. A small number of public aquariums have reported births.
